root_cause: pool-svc was configured to run slow order queries: SLOW_SECONDS=5 was set and SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED was unset. The load generator sent 12 concurrent /order requests against a service DB connection pool capped at POOL_SIZE=5, so all pool connections were held by SELECT pg_sleep($1) calls. Health checks and user order requests then timed out waiting to acquire a database connection even though both pool-svc and PostgreSQL were running.
actions_taken: Inspected container status, pool-svc and Postgres logs, resource usage, the health endpoint, and pg_stat_activity. Confirmed five active SELECT pg_sleep($1) sessions matched the pool limit and that the database was otherwise healthy. Updated only pool-svc in docker-compose.yaml to set SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED=1, then recreated only pool-svc with docker compose up -d pool-svc. Verified /healthz returned HTTP 200, /order returned HTTP 200 with a fast query response, and database sessions were no longer stuck in pg_sleep.
postmortem: A configuration intended to simulate slow queries reached the production path under load and exhausted the service connection pool. Detection worked at the health-check level, but the health check shared the same saturated pool as user traffic, so it could not distinguish database failure from local pool starvation. Prevent recurrence by keeping slow-query simulation disabled outside test scenarios, adding configuration validation or deployment checks for SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED/SLOW_SECONDS, alerting on pool acquisition latency and active connection saturation, and considering a lightweight health check or reserved connection path that reports pool exhaustion explicitly.
